g.data-narration: Welcome to Functional Units in Computer Organization. We explore the core building blocks that make computers work.
A computer contains millions of components. We organize them into functional units that perform specific tasks.
g.text: Functional units are fundamental building blocks that divide a computer's
hardware into distinct sections, each performing a specific well-defined task.

g.data-narration: There are five main functional units: ALU, Control Unit, Registers, Memory, and I/O. They work together seamlessly.
The ALU is the computational heart of the CPU. It performs arithmetic operations like addition and subtraction.
g.text: The ALU performs all arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV) and logical
operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT) on binary data.

g.data-narration: The ALU produces status flags after operations: Zero, Carry, Negative, and Overflow indicate the result's properties.
g.text: After each operation, the ALU sets special flags in the Status Register
to indicate conditions about the result.

g.data-narration: The Control Unit acts as the brain, directing all operations. It fetches, decodes, and executes instructions.
g.text: The CU coordinates all activities by generating control signals. It interprets
instructions from memory and sequences the fetch-decode-execute cycle.

g.data-narration: Registers are small, fast memory locations inside the CPU. They store temporary data for immediate processing.
g.text: Registers are the fastest memory in the computer, located inside the CPU.
They hold operands, results, and addresses during processing.

g.data-narration: Special-purpose registers serve critical functions: PC tracks next instruction, IR holds current instruction, MAR holds addresses.
g.text: Each register has a specific role in the instruction execution cycle.

g.data-narration: The Memory Unit stores programs and data. The CPU communicates with memory through MAR and MBR using read/write operations.
g.text: Main memory (RAM) stores instructions and data. The CPU accesses memory
using addresses held in MAR, with data transferred via MBR.

g.data-narration: The I/O Unit manages communication between the computer and external devices like keyboards, mice, and displays.
g.text: The I/O unit handles communication with peripheral devices through

g.data-narration: The fetch-decode-execute cycle is how CPUs process instructions. The CU fetches from memory, decodes, then executes using ALU and registers.
g.text: This fundamental cycle runs continuously to process instructions.

g.data-narration: Let's review each functional unit: ALU computes, CU controls, registers store temporarily, memory holds data, and I/O communicates externally.
In summary, functional units work in harmony: the CU directs, ALU computes, registers store, and memory holds - enabling all computation.
g.text: Functional units work in harmony: CU directs, ALU computes,
registers store temporarily, and memory holds programs.
This harmony enables all the computation in modern computers.
